text: The surgeon performs a dissection of the right recurrent laryngeal nerve during the esophagectomy, focusing on the area around the vagus nerve. The mediastinal pleura is opened along the subclavian artery, revealing the artery and nerve. Using the third arm, the pleura is held anteriorly to widen the surgical field. Gentle dissection is carried out along the vagus nerve, carefully navigating branches of nerves and vessels in the area.

The surgeon identifies the space between the recurrent laryngeal nerve and the esophagus, a common site for cancer metastasis, and aims to remove the entire tissue in this area. The recurrent laryngeal nerve is located first to prevent nerve injury during dissection.

The surgeon removes tissues surrounding the nerve, revealing lymph nodes in the right upper thoracic area. These lymph nodes, identified as 2R in the AJC system, are a common site of metastasis. The dissection continues along the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

The surgeon divides vesicular branches and identifies a nerve in the neck region, not the chest. Arteries are visible crossing the area, likely including the inferior thyroid artery. The thyroid gland is exposed and visible.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to remove tissue between the esophagus and the recurrent laryngeal nerve, extending from the chest to the neck. The dissection reveals cervical paraesophageal lymph nodes, distinct from thoracic lymph nodes.

Because robotic surgery is a very specialized method to do recurrent laryngeal lymph node dissection like this one. We can remove all of the lymph nodes completely without any difficulty. It is easy. If you are going to do this using a thoracoscopy, this may be a very, very challenging procedure. But using the robot is easier.
The surgeon achieves a clean surgical field and performs a high-level paratracheal dissection, revealing a lymph node in the surgical area.

The surgeon completes the dissection, revealing the entire course of the recurrent laryngeal nerve without any damage. All tissue between the esophagus and the recurrent laryngeal nerve, including lymph nodes, has been removed. The inferior thyroid artery is visible, marking the upper limit of the dissection.
